overviewShort: Westport straddles the Saugatuck River along Connecticut's Gold Coast, balancing a nationally recognized arts scene anchored by the Westport Country Playhouse with nearly 20 square miles of waterfront, nature preserves, and residential neighborhoods. The downtown along Main Street and Post Road draws comparisons to the best small-town commercial districts in the Northeast.

children.text: The Saugatuck River splits Westport roughly in half, and that divide shapes much of the town's geography and character. West of the river, Old Hill preserves Revolutionary- and Victorian-era homes on wooded lots. East of it, the Compo Beach and Greens Farms neighborhoods stretch toward Long Island Sound, where Sherwood Island State Park — Connecticut's first state park, designated in 1914 — anchors more than 230 acres of shoreline, wetlands, and walking trails. Two Metro-North stations serve the town: Westport station in Saugatuck and Greens Farms station to the south. Express trains reach Grand Central Terminal in roughly 70 minutes; local service runs about 80.

Downtown Westport clusters where Main Street meets the Post Road and the Saugatuck River. The commercial stretch mixes independent boutiques — Bungalow, named the most beautiful store in Connecticut by Architectural Digest — with national brands like Theory and Lululemon. Anthropologie occupies the restored 1924 Bedford Mansion in Bedford Square. Playhouse Square, adjacent to the Westport Country Playhouse, adds another node of retail and dining. Across the river, Terrain's greenhouse cafe on the Post Road serves a seasonally driven menu inside a garden retail space that has become a destination in itself.

Westport's cultural identity runs deeper than most Fairfield County towns. The Westport Country Playhouse, founded in 1931 and renovated into a state-of-the-art facility in 2005, stages a professional season that draws audiences from across the region. MoCA Westport provides visual and performing arts programming. The Levitt Pavilion on Jesup Road hosts one of the longest-running free outdoor concert series in the country, with music, dance, theater, and its signature RiverSwing evenings throughout the summer. Paul Newman lived here from 1960 until his death in 2008, and the town's creative legacy continues to attract writers, artists, and media professionals.

Schools are a primary driver of residential demand. Westport Public Schools rank as the second-best district in Connecticut, and Staples High School earns consistent top-five placement statewide with a GreatSchools rating of 10 out of 10. Bedford Middle School carries an A+ Niche grade. On the private side, Greens Farms Academy serves K-12 with an 8:1 student-to-teacher ratio and strong STEM rankings. School quality is a factor in nearly every purchase decision here.

Seven neighborhoods give Westport its range. Compo Beach offers direct Sound access and the iconic Minuteman statue marking the 1777 British landing. Coleytown borders Weston with colonial homes and the Newman-Poses Preserve. Greens Farms, the oldest section of town, has its own train station and Burying Hill Beach. Long Lots stretches north from the Post Road with properties that still include working farmland, the Fairfield County Hunt Club, and Westport's oldest house, dating to 1690. Saugatuck, the original commercial heart of Westport, has reinvented itself around waterfront dining at spots like The Whelk and Rive Bistro. The variety means nearly every buyer profile — from young families to downsizers — can find a foothold.

lifestyle:
Two Metro-North stations — Westport and Greens Farms — put the town on the New Haven Line with express service to Grand Central in about 70 minutes and locals running closer to 80. Both stations have dedicated parking, and most commuters are at a platform within ten minutes of home. I-95 and the Merritt Parkway provide highway access north toward Hartford and south toward Stamford and New York.

The dining scene has matured well beyond country-club fare. The Whelk, Bill Taibe's seafood-driven restaurant in Saugatuck, set a new standard when it opened and still anchors the waterfront strip. Rive Bistro serves French bistro cooking along the Saugatuck River. OKO brings omakase-level Japanese to the Post Road. Terrain's greenhouse cafe is as much an experience as a meal. The Westport Farmers' Market runs year-round — outdoors on Imperial Avenue in warmer months, indoors across the river through winter and spring.

Weekends revolve around the water and the arts. Compo Beach has a sandy shoreline, basketball courts, a skate park, and pickleball. Longshore Club Park operates more like a resort than a municipal facility, with an 18-hole golf course, tennis courts, a pool, a sailing school, and a marina. Sherwood Island State Park draws birdwatchers and kayakers. In the evenings, the Levitt Pavilion's free summer concert series fills the lawn, and the Westport Country Playhouse runs a professional theater season from spring through fall.
marketNotes:
Westport's median sale price crossed $2 million in 2025, and the average single-family home sold for approximately $2.75 million — up from $2.48 million the prior year. Luxury inventory concentrates above $4 million, with multiple active listings north of $10 million in waterfront and estate sections. Condominiums and townhomes remain scarce; new projects like The Gables of Westport bring limited supply at premium price points.

Among the four top Gold Coast markets — Greenwich, Westport, Darien, and New Canaan — Westport showed the most measured pace in 2025. Homes averaged about 37 days on market before going under contract, and pricing alignment mattered more than in recent years. Buyers had time to evaluate condition and value, which made accurate pricing essential for sellers. Overpriced listings lingered while well-positioned homes traded cleanly.

Entry points exist but require strategy. Condos and smaller homes near the Post Road or in Saugatuck offer the most accessible path in, though inventory is tight. For buyers targeting the $1.5 million to $3 million range, preparation and speed still matter — that segment draws the most competition from relocating families prioritizing Staples High School and the Westport school district. Above $5 million, the market rewards patience and selectivity.
highlights: Two Metro-North stations on the New Haven Line with express service to Grand Central Terminal in approximately 70 minutes
Westport Country Playhouse, founded in 1931, stages a professional theater season recognized across the region
Sherwood Island State Park preserves over 230 acres of beachfront, wetlands, and trails — Connecticut's first state park
Staples High School ranks in the top five Connecticut public high schools with a perfect 10/10 GreatSchools rating
Longshore Club Park provides public access to an 18-hole golf course, tennis courts, pool, sailing school, and marina
Downtown Main Street and Bedford Square mix independent boutiques with national brands in a walkable riverside setting
Levitt Pavilion hosts one of the nation's longest-running free outdoor performing arts series each summer
seoTitle: Westport CT Real Estate | Luxury Homes & Arts Culture
seoDescription: Explore Westport, Connecticut homes from waterfront estates to village neighborhoods. Schools, commute times, and market data. Contact Matt Caiola today.

faqs.question: How long is the train commute from Westport to New York City?
faqs.answer: Metro-North express trains from Westport station reach Grand Central Terminal in approximately 70 minutes. Local service takes closer to 80 minutes. The town also has a second station at Greens Farms, which adds roughly 5 to 10 minutes to the trip depending on the schedule. Both stations offer commuter parking with free access evenings and weekends.

faqs.question: What are the main neighborhoods in Westport, CT?
faqs.answer: Westport has seven primary neighborhoods: Compo Beach (waterfront with direct Sound access), Coleytown (wooded colonials bordering Weston), Downtown Westport (Main Street shops and restaurants along the Saugatuck River), Greens Farms (the oldest section with its own train station and Burying Hill Beach), Long Lots (larger properties stretching north toward Fairfield), Old Hill (historic homes west of the river), and Saugatuck (the original commercial center, now a dining and waterfront destination).

faqs.question: What are home prices in Westport, Connecticut?
faqs.answer: The median sale price in Westport is approximately $2 million, and the average single-family home sells for around $2.75 million. Condominiums and townhomes offer more accessible entry points but are limited in supply. Waterfront estates and properties in premium locations regularly list above $5 million, with some exceeding $10 million. Pricing varies significantly by neighborhood and proximity to the water.

faqs.question: How are Westport public schools ranked?
faqs.answer: Westport Public Schools rank as the second-best school district in Connecticut. Staples High School places in the top five statewide and holds a 10 out of 10 GreatSchools rating along with an A+ Niche grade. Bedford Middle School also carries an A+ overall grade. The private option, Greens Farms Academy, serves K through 12 with an 8:1 student-to-teacher ratio and strong STEM programs.

faqs.question: What cultural attractions distinguish Westport from other Fairfield County towns?
faqs.answer: Westport has one of the strongest arts scenes on the Gold Coast. The Westport Country Playhouse, founded in 1931, produces a professional theater season. MoCA Westport offers visual and performing arts year-round. The Levitt Pavilion stages one of the longest-running free outdoor concert series in the country each summer, with music, dance, and theater. The town's creative legacy traces back decades — Paul Newman was a longtime resident and driving force behind the Playhouse.
